clean_story: Invisible to radar.  Shapes that trick waves. "Stealth aircraft geometry" means designing planes with angles and surfaces that scatter radar signals—like a mirror bouncing light away from your eyes. Radar works by sending radio waves that bounce off objects, revealing their location. But stealth planes use flat panels and sharp edges to redirect those waves elsewhere—like skipping a stone so it never returns to you. The F-117 Nighthawk’s faceted body looks jagged for a reason:Each flat surface is angled to reflect radar energy upward or sideways, not back to the sender. Even the engine intakes are hidden behind grids to block radar from seeing inside. The B-2 Spirit takes this further—its smooth, wing-like shape blends curves and materials to absorb or deflect waves, making it nearly invisible. No single angle gives it away. Three related subjects are radar evasion, aerodynamic trade-offs and metamaterial cloaking.
